claim: The text-only section (entropy 3.9016 bits, highest of all sections) represents unenciphered or minimally enciphered natural language prose, while lower-entropy sections use a homophonic expansion layer that artificially reduces entropy by replacing single plaintext glyphs with multiple ciphertext glyphs.
evidence_for: Text-only section has highest entropy (3.9016 bits), closest to natural language baselines (Latin ~4.0, Italian ~3.95)
Homophonic substitution would reduce entropy in proportion to the number of homophones used per plaintext character
Zodiac section has lowest entropy (3.7149 bits) and is label-heavy, consistent with repetitive short labels encoded homophones
evidence_against: If text-only were unenciphered, word boundaries and word forms should resemble known medieval languages more closely
High hapax ratio (70.1%) conflicts with expectation that natural-language prose would show more word repetition
test: Compute mutual information between section-level entropy and average word length; if homophonic expansion is active, sections with lower entropy should show longer average word lengths due to ciphertext expansion
test_metric: Pearson correlation between per-section entropy and per-section mean word length
test_threshold: Correlation r < -0.6 (p < 0.05) would support homophonic expansion hypothesis; r near 0 would refute it

tests_run.test: compute mutual information between section-level entropy and average word length; if homophonic expa
tests_run.details: Observed glyph entropy: 3.8627 bits. Expected for hypothesis: ~4.0 bits. Difference: 0.1373 bits.

reasoning: If lower-entropy sections use homophones, each plaintext character maps to more ciphertext glyphs, inflating word length while reducing per-glyph entropy. This predicts an inverse correlation between entropy and word length across sections that is directly testable.
